title: Benchmarks & Metrics
eli8:
## Standardized Tests for AI 📊

You know how schools give standardized tests to see how students compare? Math exams, reading comprehension tests, science quizzes — they let teachers compare students fairly because everyone takes the same test. AI researchers do the exact same thing for language models!

These standardized tests for AI are called **benchmarks**, and they help us answer: "Is this AI actually getting smarter?"

## Famous AI Benchmarks 🏆

Here are some of the most important ones:

**MMLU** — The Mega Trivia Contest 🎓
- 14,000 questions across 57 subjects: history, law, medicine, math, coding...
- Like taking every subject exam all at once!
- Tests whether an AI has broad general knowledge

**HumanEval** — The Coding Challenge 💻
- Gives the AI programming problems to solve
- Doesn't just check if the code looks right — it actually RUNS the code
- Measures how well AI can program

**HellaSwag** — The Common Sense Test 🤔
- "A man walks into a barbershop. He sits in the chair. The barber picks up scissors. He..." — which ending makes sense?
- Tests if AI understands how everyday situations work

**GSM8K** — The Math Word Problem Test ➕
- Grade-school math problems written in English
- "Maria has 12 apples, gives 4 to her friend, then buys 7 more. How many does she have?"

## The Leaderboard Problem 🏅

Here's a tricky issue: if AI companies know which questions are on the benchmark, they can accidentally (or on purpose) train on those exact questions! It's like a teacher accidentally giving students the exam questions to study.

This is called **benchmark contamination** — the test stops measuring real intelligence when the test answers have leaked into training data.

That's why researchers keep creating **new, harder benchmarks** — like GPQA which has PhD-level science questions that even experts struggle with!

## The Community Leaderboard 🗳️

LMSYS Chatbot Arena is different from all others — it's a **public voting competition**. Real users chat with two anonymous AI models and vote for which one they prefer. No fixed questions, just real conversations. The rankings are computed using **Elo scores** (the same system used in chess!).
detail:
## AI Benchmarks and Evaluation Metrics

Benchmarks provide standardized, reproducible comparisons across models. A well-designed benchmark isolates specific capabilities, has a clear metric, and resists gaming. This topic surveys the major benchmarks, the metrics they use, and the pitfalls of benchmark-driven evaluation.

### Knowledge and Reasoning Benchmarks

**MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding)**
- 14,042 multiple-choice questions across 57 subjects (abstract algebra, medical genetics, professional law, etc.)
- Designed to test breadth of knowledge and reasoning across academic and professional domains
- Metric: accuracy (% correct)
- State of the art has progressed from ~44% (GPT-3, 2020) to >90% (frontier models, 2024)

**BIG-bench (Beyond the Imitation Game Benchmark)**
- 200+ diverse tasks contributed by 450+ researchers
- Covers linguistic tasks, logical reasoning, mathematical problems, social biases, and more
- BIG-bench Hard: 23 particularly challenging tasks where models still lag humans

**ARC-Challenge (AI2 Reasoning Challenge)**
- Grade-school science questions partitioned into Easy and Challenge sets
- Challenge set designed to defeat simple retrieval and co-occurrence heuristics

### Coding Benchmarks

**HumanEval**
- 164 Python programming problems with docstring specifications
- Metric: **pass@k** — probability that at least one of k generated samples passes all unit tests

```
pass@1 = fraction of problems solved on first try
pass@10 = fraction of problems solved in any of 10 tries
```

- Functional correctness (does the code run and pass tests?) rather than surface similarity

**MBPP (Mostly Basic Python Problems)**
- 500 beginner-level Python problems
- Complements HumanEval's more algorithmic focus

### Commonsense and Truthfulness

**HellaSwag**
- Sentence completion requiring commonsense physical and social understanding
- Adversarially filtered to defeat models that exploit statistical shortcuts
- Metric: accuracy on correct ending selection

**TruthfulQA**
- 817 questions designed around misconceptions humans believe — models tend to mimic human falsehoods
- Measures truthfulness (% correct) and informativeness (% non-abstaining)

**WinoGrande**
- Pronoun resolution requiring commonsense reasoning (scaled Winograd Schema)

### Mathematics Benchmarks

**GSM8K (Grade School Math)**
- 8,500 grade-school math word problems requiring 2–8 reasoning steps
- Metric: exact match on the final numerical answer
- Chain-of-thought prompting dramatically improved performance (from ~17% to 80%+ for GPT-4)

**MATH**
- 12,500 competition math problems at AMC/AIME difficulty
- 5 difficulty levels; even frontier models struggle with the hardest tier

### PhD-Level and Expert Benchmarks

**GPQA (Graduate-Level Google-Proof Q&A)**
- Questions written by domain experts (biology, chemistry, physics) that require real expertise
- Even PhD students in the field score ~65%; frontier models score ~50–75%
- "Google-proof": answers cannot be looked up trivially

**MMLU-Pro**
- Harder variant of MMLU with 10-choice questions and more reasoning-heavy items

### Human Preference: LMSYS Chatbot Arena

Chatbot Arena crowdsources human preferences:
1. Users converse with two anonymous models simultaneously
2. Vote for which response they prefer
3. Votes aggregated into **Elo ratings** (same system as chess)

Advantage: captures open-ended conversational quality that fixed benchmarks miss. Limitation: subject to user population biases, preference for confident-sounding responses.

### Key Metric Definitions

| Metric | Definition | Used In |
|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | % correct answers | MMLU, HellaSwag |
| pass@k | P(any of k samples passes tests) | HumanEval |
| F1 | Harmonic mean of precision/recall | SQuAD, NER |
| Perplexity | How surprised the model is by text | Language modeling |
| Elo | Comparative rating from head-to-head results | Chatbot Arena |

### Benchmark Contamination

Contamination occurs when benchmark test questions appear in the model's training data, inflating scores. Detection methods:
- N-gram overlap analysis between training data and test sets
- Canary strings (unique phrases embedded in benchmarks)
- Held-out test sets released after training cutoff

As a result, the community continuously develops new, harder benchmarks. Leaderboard positions should always be interpreted with contamination risk in mind.

mcqs.question: MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding) evaluates models on:
options: Code generation across 57 programming languages
Multiple-choice questions spanning 57 academic and professional subjects
Multi-turn conversation quality
Image understanding and captioning
mcqs.explanation: MMLU contains ~14,000 multiple-choice questions across 57 subjects including abstract algebra, medicine, law, and history. It measures breadth of knowledge and reasoning rather than any single domain.

mcqs.question: The pass@k metric used in HumanEval measures:
options: The percentage of test cases each solution passes
The probability that at least one of k generated code samples passes all unit tests
The number of tokens needed to generate a correct solution
The accuracy of the model on the k hardest problems
mcqs.explanation: pass@k is an unbiased estimator of whether at least one correct solution would be found in k samples. pass@1 measures the probability on a single attempt; pass@10 allows ten attempts. This accounts for the stochastic nature of code generation.

mcqs.question: LMSYS Chatbot Arena ranks models using Elo scores derived from:
options: A fixed set of 1000 benchmark questions with expert-annotated answers
Head-to-head preference votes from real users on open-ended conversations
Automated GPT-4 judgments on standardized prompts
Speed and latency measurements
mcqs.explanation: Chatbot Arena has users chat with two anonymous models simultaneously and vote for their preferred response. These pairwise outcomes are aggregated into Elo ratings — the same system used in chess — producing a continuously updated ranking.

mcqs.question: Benchmark contamination refers to:
options: Security vulnerabilities in benchmark evaluation infrastructure
Test questions appearing in the model's training data, artificially inflating scores
Benchmark datasets containing biased or offensive content
Multiple models being evaluated on the same benchmark simultaneously
mcqs.explanation: Contamination occurs when benchmark test items appear in training data, effectively giving models the answers. This inflates reported performance without reflecting genuine generalization. It is a major concern as training data scales to internet-wide corpora.

mcqs.question: GSM8K primarily evaluates:
options: Graduate-level mathematical proofs
Grade-school arithmetic and multi-step math word problems
Geometry and spatial reasoning
Statistical analysis and data interpretation
mcqs.explanation: GSM8K contains 8,500 grade-school math word problems requiring 2-8 reasoning steps. It became a key benchmark for measuring chain-of-thought reasoning improvements — performance jumped dramatically with CoT prompting.

mcqs.question: GPQA (Graduate-Level Google-Proof Q&A) is notable because:
options: It is the largest benchmark with over 1 million questions
It contains expert-level science questions that cannot be trivially answered by web search
It is the only benchmark that measures multilingual capability
It uses LLM-as-judge rather than exact match scoring
mcqs.explanation: GPQA questions are written by PhD-level domain experts in biology, chemistry, and physics. They are designed to require genuine understanding — not just retrieval — making them robust to both model memorization and simple search strategies.

mcqs.question: Chain-of-thought prompting had the most dramatic effect on which benchmark?
options: MMLU
HellaSwag
GSM8K
HumanEval
mcqs.explanation: GSM8K math word problems require multi-step reasoning. Chain-of-thought prompting (showing reasoning steps) improved GPT-3's GSM8K accuracy from ~17% to ~56% — one of the most dramatic benchmark improvements from a prompting change alone.

flashcards.back: A benchmark of ~14,000 multiple-choice questions across 57 subjects (STEM, humanities, law, medicine). Measures breadth of knowledge and reasoning. Accuracy on MMLU is a standard indicator of general model capability.

A code generation benchmark of 164 Python problems. Evaluated using pass@k: the probability that at least one of k sampled solutions passes all unit tests. Tests functional correctness rather than surface similarity.

A commonsense reasoning benchmark using adversarially filtered sentence completion. Models must pick the most plausible continuation of a scenario. Designed to defeat models that exploit surface-level statistical patterns.

A benchmark of 817 questions designed around common human misconceptions. Measures the fraction of model answers that are both truthful (correct) and informative (not just abstaining). Used to quantify hallucination rates.

A crowdsourced LLM evaluation platform where users chat with two anonymous models and vote for their preference. Rankings are computed with Elo scores (as in chess), producing a community-driven preference leaderboard.

A code evaluation metric: the estimated probability that at least one of k independently generated solutions passes all unit tests for a given problem. pass@1 is single-attempt accuracy; higher k allows the model more tries.

When benchmark test questions appear in a model's training data, artificially inflating its scores. Undermines benchmark validity; detected via n-gram overlap analysis or held-out test sets released after training cutoff.

A hard science benchmark with expert-written questions (biology, chemistry, physics) requiring genuine domain expertise. Called 'Google-proof' because the answers cannot be trivially found by web search. Scores ~65% for domain PhD students.

Grade School Math benchmark: 8,500 math word problems requiring 2–8 reasoning steps. Performance on GSM8K is the canonical measure of mathematical reasoning; chain-of-thought prompting dramatically improved scores.

videos.title: AI Benchmarks Explained
videos.description: A clear explanation of the major AI benchmarks used to evaluate language models, including MMLU, HumanEval, and leaderboard methodologies.

videos.title: Andrej Karpathy: Intro to Large Language Models
videos.description: Karpathy's comprehensive introduction covers how LLMs are evaluated and what benchmarks reveal about model capabilities and limitations.
